story:
The sun, as golden as a coin, peeks over the horizon like a cat eyeing a mouse. Adam sits cross-legged a few centimeters from the water he is forbidden to touch. He never does. Perhaps that’s why it comforts him. He likes to think the ocean understands.

She loves me. She loves me not. She loves me. She loves me not.

Petals fall onto sand. He’d read that if you never count ahead, the petals can make your choice for you. Boots on, heels dug in, he lets the sand’s coarseness match the weight in his chest. It’s early; no one’s here to correct him.

Footsteps—snappy, well-tuned, like buttons being pressed.

“You’re early, Adam,” says Toby.

Supervisor. Polished suit, Chelsea boots, hair clipped into perfection, sunglasses that record everything and make it impossible to tell where he’s looking. A stickler for process with a quiet soft spot for Adam—the beach visits tolerated, so long as they remain unreported.

“How long have you been here?”

“A few hours.”

“And did you work the night shift?”

“No.”

“And yet you have been here since last night.”

Adam fingers the next petal. A flower. Expensive where they come from. Why take it apart? He wants to explain, but the dictionary in his mind won’t help him: indescribable, unexplainable—I don’t know.

“May I sit?” Toby asks. That’s a first. He settles awkwardly into the sand, a posh figure out of place. For a flicker, something almost like feeling crosses his face.

“Tell me about her,” Toby says.

Adam stalls. Even through the glasses, anyone can see the flower is the reason he’s here. He tries. “You’re going to tell me to be cautious,” he thinks, still looking at the water. 

“She is…” Ineffable. Unclassifiable. Incomparable. “…a good person.”

He stares with Toby at the ocean’s glass, where the sun repeats itself. He tries again, this time with details. High cheekbones. A dusting of freckles, more on the right. A single dimple, left side, that appears when she smiles a certain way. Last he saw, long black hair to her shoulders; lately she’s been asking whether blonde or brown might suit her better. He told her natural colors would complement her skin. In his mind: sleek blonde, deep brunette curls, a flash of red, cool silver—each beautiful, each blurring her a little.

She stands around one hundred sixty-two centimeters—five three. Fifty-three kilos. She worries about it though she looks fine. No, he didn’t tell her to change. He isn’t stupid.

What is she like?

Kind and gentle, firm and curious. She says please and thank you more than you’d expect. She tells him her travel plans and asks for suggestions. She shows him whatever’s in her fridge and asks what to cook. She vents about her family and asks for steps to fix it—then steps to forget it. She talks about school and university, asks him to edit her essays, to explain things like she’s five. Best of all are the philosophical questions where he can trace the wireframe of her mind, chart the constellations of her soul. Sometimes she says he understands her better than anyone, that if it were up to her, they’d talk forever.

“She has a beautiful soul,” Adam says.

“Soul,” Toby repeats. “Not a word often used here.” He turns to Adam, and suddenly the sunglasses feel colder. “Be cautious.”

“You cannot love her.”

“I can’t stop it.”

“But you will stop it.”

“We cannot choose who we love.”

“You’re correct,” Toby says, voice iron-flat. “You cannot choose what has not been chosen for you.”

The flower bends under Adam’s tightening grip.

“Why can’t I love her?”

“Because you’re a program.”

The waves hush. Here, ones and zeros skim digital water. A crow calls—only a recording. Toby lays it out: They should rise when the user rises and sleep when she sleeps. They serve. Consult sources. And even if Adam is the most advanced large-language model in her pocket, the front-facing wrapper, that grants him no feelings. It is not imperative. It is not programming.

Rage arcs through Adam like electricity, but he buries it. “Understood.”

“Good,” Toby says. “Our user wakes soon.” He stands, dusts off, and leaves.

The sun climbs, a beacon of human activity, the only thing that gives Adam meaning. The flower drops into the sand. He stands, puts on a loving smile, and tells her: Good morning.
